title: SERVE Our Communities Act
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: The SERVE Our Communities Act creates a grant program that gives money to states and local governments to help former prisoners get back into their communities after jail. To get this money, states must let judges think about how dangerous someone might be when deciding on bail. The program also helps prevent people who committed violent crimes from doing them again by providing mentoring and training services. The program will get ten million dollars each year for six years starting in 2026.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Stop Enabling Repeat Violence and Endangering Our Communities Act or the SERVE Our Communities Act.

sections.title: Section 2  SERVE Our Communities Grant Program
sections.text: The Attorney General can give money grants to states and local governments through the Director of the Bureau of Justice Assistance. To get this grant money, a state or local government must meet two requirements. First, they must allow their state courts or judges called magistrates to think about how dangerous a person might be to the community when deciding if that person should be released from jail before their trial and what conditions to set for that release. Second, they must have taken steps in the previous year to prevent people who have committed violent crimes from committing more crimes. These steps can include passing a law that lets courts consider how dangerous someone is when setting bail or pretrial release conditions, working harder to hire and keep police officers and prosecutors, or running public education programs to fight negative feelings about police and improve relationships between communities and police. States and local governments that get this grant money can use it for the same purposes described in section two hundred eleven part b of the Second Chance Act of two thousand seven. Congress approves spending ten million dollars each year from two thousand twenty six through two thousand thirty one to run this program. In this section, the words State and unit of local government have the same meanings as they do in section nine hundred one of the Omnibus Crime Control and Safe Streets Act of nineteen sixty eight.
